critic_raw:
All preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting, explicitly stated: 'all preprocessing transformers...are fit on the entire dataset.' For the text tokenizer, this means the vocabulary is built from the full 24-month feedback corpus including months 19-24 (the test period with patients who have no prior feedback in training). The vocabulary built from all patients' text implicitly encodes test-patient language patterns into the embedding matrix indices. When the model encounters test-patient feedback, it processes tokens whose frequency statistics were derived from a corpus that included those patients' own submissions.

Prior adherence rate is defined as a 'rolling 6-month average' and listed as a feature. At prediction time in the deployment scenario (months 19-24 test period), a patient's 6-month rolling adherence is computed from months 13-18 (overlapping with the validation period). If validation-period adherence data is used to compute this feature for test patients, and if the patient's adherence trajectory in months 13-18 was influenced by the same external events that affect months 19-24 adherence, this feature carries information about the test-period outcome's context without strictly leaking the label. The design does not specify how this rolling feature is computed at the validation/test boundary.

The test set specifically uses 'patients with no prior feedback in training or validation.' This is a distribution shift by design: the test population is specifically new patients (those who gave no feedback in months 1-18), while the training population is established patients (months 1-12 feedback submitters). New patients and established patients likely have different adherence characteristics and different feedback language patterns. The model is trained on established patients and evaluated on new patients — this is not a deployment simulation but a generalization-to-new-patients test, and the stated hypothesis (RNN outperforms VADER baseline) is confounded by this population shift.

The VADER sentiment baseline is a fixed lexicon-based system not trained on clinical/medication adherence feedback text; it was developed on social media data. VADER's compound scores for clinical adherence feedback text may have poor calibration. Using VADER as the baseline without first validating its sentiment-adherence correlation on the target domain sets a potentially weak baseline that can be beaten by the RNN for reasons unrelated to the RNN's sequential modeling advantage.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing tokenizer vocabulary is built from the full 24-month corpus before splitting, encoding test-patient language patterns into the vocabulary and embedding statistics used during training.

Prior adherence rate (6-month rolling average) for test-period patients overlaps with the validation period; the boundary computation is unspecified and may carry validation-era adherence trajectory information into the test feature.

The test set is specifically restricted to patients with no prior feedback in months 1-18 (new patients), while training uses established patients — this creates a systematic population distribution shift that confounds the comparison between models with the model's ability to generalize to new patients.

VADER was developed on social media data and its calibration for clinical adherence feedback is unvalidated; any RNN advantage may reflect VADER's domain mismatch rather than the RNN's sequential modeling capacity.

The test set is specifically restricted to patients with no prior feedback in months 1-18 (new patients), while training uses established patients — this creates a systematic population distribution shift that confounds the comparison between models with the model's ability to generalize to new patients.

VADER was developed on social media data and its calibration for clinical adherence feedback is unvalidated; any RNN advantage may reflect VADER's domain mismatch rather than the RNN's sequential modeling capacity.
